summary: This abstract outlines the mechanisms of innate immune recognition during viral infection. It highlights that host cells utilize two distinct classes of pattern-recognition receptors—Toll-like receptors (TLRs) and cytoplasmic RNA helicases—which operate in different intracellular compartments to detect viral components and trigger antiviral responses.

principles.statement: The antiviral innate immune response relies on multiple classes of pattern-recognition receptors operating in distinct intracellular compartments.
principles.rationale: Toll-like receptors and cytoplasmic RNA helicases both recognize viral components (like nucleic acids and double-stranded RNA) but do so in different parts of the cell and via distinct signaling pathways to ensure a robust type I interferon response.
principles.source_quote: These two classes of pattern-recognition receptor molecules are expressed in different intracellular compartments and induce type I interferon responses via distinct signaling pathways.
